content.title: Mammoth kill
content.summary: {'A': '这段话主要讲述了猛犸象的体型、犬牙、生长和社会结构等方面的内容。', 'B': '这段话主要讲述了北美曾经属于猛犸象、骆驼、体型如牛般巨大的地懒、熊大小的海狸和其他强大的野兽，然而大约11000年前，这些大型哺乳动物和其他约70个物种消失了，而他们的消失与人类抵达新世界和气候剧变有关，尽管经过几十年的科学调查，但确切的原因仍然是个谜。现在新的发现支持了其中一个有争议的假设：人类狩猎导致了这些巨型动物的灭绝。', 'C': '这段话主要讲述了如果人类每年杀死15到20只大型哺乳动物，人类可以在1000年内消灭动物种群，尤其是大型哺乳动物更容易受到压力的影响。', 'D': '这段话主要讲述了对于Alroy的评估并不是每个人都同意，其中一个原因是结果在一定程度上依赖于对灭绝动物的种群规模估计，而这些数字并不一定可靠。此外，美国自然历史博物馆的哺乳动物学家Ross D. E. MacPhee指出，相关的考古记录中只有几个石制尖头嵌入猛犸象骨骼中的例子（而且需要注意的是，其他巨型动物遗骸中并没有发现这种情况），这与如果狩猎导致这些动物灭绝的说法相去甚远。此外，一些物种的分布范围非常广阔，比如巨型杰斐逊地懒，它生活在北至育空地区，南至墨西哥，这使得大规模屠杀足以导致它们灭绝的说法相当不可信，他说。', 'E': '这段话主要讲述了人类可能通过引入超致命疾病（可能是通过狗或寄生虫）间接导致了新世界物种的灭绝，这种疾病可能会迅速传播并导致大型哺乳动物种群难以恢复，最终推动它们走向无法挽回的境地。虽然目前还没有实证证据支持超致命疾病假设，但作者希望通过对最后几只灭绝的猛犸象的组织和DNA进行分析，最终揭示出致命微生物的存在。', 'F': '这段话主要讲述了北美动物灭绝的第三种解释，即归咎于气候变化，而非人类活动。气候不稳定导致某些栖息地消失，物种分散，对于某些动物来说，这种变化带来了机会，但对于大多数大型动物来说，环境的同质化使它们的地理范围缩小，这对于需要大范围活动的大型动物来说是致命的。最后一次重大气候波动将它们推向灭绝的边缘。与此同时，Alroy则坚信是人类猎人导致了冰河时代的巨兽灭绝，他认为过度捕杀模型解释了疾病和气候情景解释的一切，并对最终灭绝的物种做出了准确的预测。'}

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: Like their modern relatives, mammoths were quite large. The largest known species reached heights in the region of 4 m at the shoulder and weights of up to 8 tonnes, while exceptionally large males may have exceeded 12 tonnes. However, most species of mammoth were only about as large as a modern Asian elephant. Both sexes bore tusks. A first, small set appeared at about the age of six months, and these were replaced at about 18 months by the permanent set. Growth of the permanent set was at a rate of about 2.5 to 15.2 cm per year. Based on studies of their close relatives, the modern elephants, mammoths probably had a gestation period of 22 months, resulting in a single calf being born. Their social structure was probably the same as that of African and Asian elephants, with females living in herds headed by a matriarch, whilst bulls lived solitary lives or formed loose groups after sexual maturity.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text:
MEXICO CITY – Although it's hard to imagine in this age of urban sprawl and automobiles, North America once belonged to mammoths, camels, ground sloths as large as cows, bear-size beavers and other formidable beasts. Some 11,000 years ago, however, these large-bodied mammals and others – about 70 species in all – disappeared. Their demise coincided roughly with the arrival of humans in the New World and dramatic climatic change – factors that have inspired several theories about the die-off. Yet despite decades of scientific investigation, the exact cause remains a mystery. Now new findings offer support to one of these controversial hypotheses: that human hunting drove this megafaunal menagerie to extinction. The overkill model emerged in the 1960s, when it was put forth by Paul S. Martin of the University of
Arizona. Since then, critics have charged that no evidence exists to support the idea that the first Americans hunted to the extent necessary to cause these extinctions. But at the annual meeting of the Society of Vertebrate Paleontology in Mexico City last October, paleoecologist John Alroy of the University of California at Santa Barbara argued that, in fact, hunting-driven extinction is not only plausible, it was unavoidable. He has determined, using a computer simulation, that even a very modest amount of hunting would have wiped these animals out.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: Assuming an initial human population of 100 people that grew no more than 2 percent annually, Alroy determined that if each band of, say, 50 people killed 15 to 20 large mammals a year, humans could have eliminated the animal populations within 1,000 years. Large mammals in particular would have been vulnerable to the pressure because they have longer gestation periods than smaller mammals and their young require extended care.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: Not everyone agrees with Alroy's assessment. For one, the results depend in part on population-size estimates for the extinct animals – figures that are not necessarily reliable. But a more specific criticism comes from mammalogist Ross D. E. MacPhee of the American Museum of Natural History in New York City, who points out that the relevant archaeological record contains barely a dozen examples of stone points embedded in mammoth bones (and none, it should be noted, are known from other megafaunal remains) – hardly what one might expect if hunting drove these animals to extinction. Furthermore, some of these species had huge ranges – the giant Jefferson's ground sloth, for example, lived as far north as the Yukon and as far south as Mexico – which would have made slaughtering them in numbers sufficient to cause their extinction rather implausible, he says.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: MacPhee agrees that humans most likely brought about these extinctions (as well as others around the world that coincided with human arrival), but not directly. Rather he suggests that people may have introduced hyperlethal disease, perhaps through their dogs or hitchhiking vermin, which then spread wildly among the immunologically naive species of the New World. As in the overkill model, populations of large mammals would have a harder time recovering. Repeated outbreaks of a hyperdisease could thus quickly drive them to the point of no return. So far MacPhee does not have empirical evidence for the hyperdisease hypothesis, and it won't be easy to come by: hyperlethal disease would kill far too quickly to leave its signature on the bones themselves. But he hopes that analyses of tissue and DNA from the last mammoths to perish will eventually reveal murderous microbes.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: The third explanation for what brought on this North American extinction does not involve human beings. Instead its proponents blame the loss on the weather. The Pleistocene epoch witnessed considerable climatic instability, explains paleontologist Russell W. Graham of the Denver Museum of Nature and Science. As a result, certain habitats disappeared, and species that had once formed communities split apart. For some animals, this change brought opportunity. For much of the megafauna, however, the increasingly homogeneous environment left them with shrinking geographical ranges – a death sentence for large animals, which need large ranges. Although these creatures managed to maintain viable populations through most of the Pleistocene, the final major fluctuation – the so-called Younger Dryas event – pushed them over the edge, Graham says. For his part, Alroy is convinced that human hunters demolished the titans of the Ice Age. The overkill model explains everything the disease and climate scenarios explain, he asserts, and makes accurate predictions about which species would eventually go extinct. "Personally, I'm a vegetarian," he remarks, "and I find all of this kind of gross – but believable."

questions.analysis:
要快速得到答案"(considerable) climatic instability"，我们可以通过以下步骤来定位和选择答案。

首先，我们可以注意到题干中提到了Graham提出了一个第三个假设。我们可以在原文中找到与之相关的信息。原文中提到了一个第三个解释，它与人类无关，而是将灭绝归咎于天气。这个解释由古生物学家Russell W. Graham提出。他解释说，更新世纪经历了相当大的气候不稳定性。

接下来，我们可以在原文中找到与气候不稳定性相关的信息。原文中提到，由于气候不稳定性，某些栖息地消失了，曾经形成群落的物种分离了。这种变化对一些动物来说带来了机会，但对大部分大型动物来说，环境越来越同质化导致它们的地理范围缩小，这对大型动物来说是一个死亡判决。

因此，我们可以得出结论，第三个假设中提到的因素是"climatic instability"，即气候不稳定性。

所以，答案是"(considerable) climatic instability"。

题干中提到了Graham提出了一个第三个假设，这个假设解释了在更新世时期，某些物种的消失，这给这些巨兽带来了危险信号，并最终导致它们灭绝。我们可以通过定位词和同义表达来快速找到答案。

首先，我们可以通过定位词"Graham"来定位到相关原文中的同义表达"Russell W. Graham"。在这句话中，Russell W. Graham解释了更新世时期的气候不稳定性。

然后，我们可以通过定位词"third hypothesis"来定位到相关原文中的同义表达"The third explanation"。在这句话中，第三个解释并不涉及人类。

接下来，我们可以通过定位词"Pleistocene epoch"来定位到相关原文中的同义表达"The Pleistocene epoch"。在这句话中，Russell W. Graham解释了更新世时期的气候不稳定性。

然后，我们可以通过定位词"species disappear"来定位到相关原文中的同义表达"species that had once formed communities split apart"。在这句话中，某些物种的栖息地消失了，曾经形成群落的物种分散了。

接着，我们可以通过定位词"reduced"来定位到相关原文中的同义表达"shrinking"。在这句话中，越来越同质化的环境使得这些巨兽的地理范围缩小，对于需要大范围的大型动物来说，这是一个死亡判决。

最后，我们可以通过定位词"finally wiped them out"来定位到相关原文中的同义表达"pushed them over the edge"。在这句话中，尽管这些生物在大部分更新世时期都能维持可行的种群，但最后的重大波动——所谓的Younger Dryas事件——使它们走到了灭绝的边缘。

题干要求我们找出解释哪个物种最终会消失的人类狩猎的解释。我们可以通过以下步骤快速找到答案"John Alroy"：

questions.title: Illness rather than hunting caused extensive extinction.
questions.analysis:
题干中提到了疾病导致了广泛的灭绝。在原文中，作者提到了一种可能的假设，即人类通过狗或寄生虫引入了一种超致命的疾病，这种疾病在新大陆的免疫系统不成熟的物种中迅速传播。这种超致命疾病的多次爆发可能会迅速将大型哺乳动物的种群推向无法恢复的地步。因此，我们可以得出结论，疾病导致了广泛的灭绝。

根据题干中的关键词"illness"，我们可以在原文中找到同义表达"hyperlethal disease"。而在原文中提到这个假设的作者是Ross D.E. MacPhee。因此，答案是"Ross D.E. MacPhee"。

所以，我们可以通过在原文中找到与题干中关键词的同义表达，并结合作者的名字来快速得到答案"Ross D.E. MacPhee"。

central_sentences.sentence: MacPhee agrees that humans most likely brought about these extinctions (as well as others around the world that coincided with human arrival), but not directly. Rather he suggests that people may have introduced hyperlethal disease, perhaps through their dogs or hitchhiking vermin, which then spread wildly among the immunologically naive species of the New World. As in the overkill model, populations of large mammals would have a harder time recovering. Repeated outbreaks of a hyperdisease could thus quickly drive them to the point of no return. So far MacPhee does not have empirical evidence for the hyperdisease hypothesis, and it won’t be easy to come by: hyperlethal disease would kill far too quickly to leave its signature on the bones themselves

content.title: Stress of Workplace
content.summary: {'A': '大多数高级主管的工作量在极度繁忙和疯狂之间摇摆不定，平均每周工作60小时。', 'B': '这段话主要讲述了工作负荷过重的三个警示信号：睡眠、日程安排和家庭。当Neil Plumridge连续三个晚上睡眠不足六个小时、不断需要重新安排约会以及错过家庭生日或纪念日时，他知道自己的工作负荷过重。应对压力的关键不是寻求缓解，而是重新评估工作负荷。', 'C': '这段话主要讲述了Vanessa Stoykov经营自己的广告和公关业务多年，公司快速发展，她需要集中精力缓解短期压力，而不是将更多工作委派给他人。', 'D': '这段话主要讲了高级主管和其他高潜力商业人士在繁重工作负荷下蓬勃发展的原因以及应对压力的个体差异。', 'E': '这段话主要讲了积极心理学对压力管理的影响，指出积极的体验可以增强人们的适应能力，而冥想和瑜伽等方法可以帮助人们调节压力反应并掌控自己的情绪。', 'F': '人们通常因为对自己设定了不切实际的期望而感到压力，而过度承诺是一种个人问题，可能是人们天生就有这种倾向。', 'G': '这段话主要讲了人们总是相信未来会比现在更不忙，但这是一种误解，因为人们往往低估了未来任务的完成时间，并且难以想象未来时间的竞争压力。'}

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: How busy is too busy? For some it means having to miss the occasional long lunch; for others it means missing lunch altogether. For a few, it is not being able to take a "sickie" once a month. Then there is a group of people for whom working every evening and weekend is normal, and frantic is the tempo of their lives. For most senior executives, workloads swing between extremely busy and frenzied. The vice-president of the management consultancy AT Kearney and its head of telecommunications for the Asia-Pacific region, Neil Plumridge, says his work weeks vary from a "manageable" 45 hours to 80 hours, but average 60 hours.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: Three warning signs alert Plumridge about his workload: sleep, scheduling and family. He knows he has too much on when he gets less than six hours of sleep for three consecutive nights; when he is constantly having to reschedule appointments; "and the third one is on the family side", says Plumridge, the father of a three-year-old daughter, and expecting a second child in October. "If I happen to miss a birthday or anniversary, I know things are out of control."Being "too busy" is highly subjective. But for any individual, the perception of being too busy over a prolonged period can start showing up as stress: disturbed sleep, and declining mental and physical health. National workers' compensation figures show stress causes the most lost time of any workplace injury. Employees suffering stress are off work an average of 16.6 weeks. The effects of stress are also expensive. Comcare, the Federal Government insurer, reports that in 2003-04, claims for psychological injury accounted for 7% of claims but almost 27% of claim costs. Experts say the key to dealing with stress is not to focus on relief – a game of golf or a massage – but to reassess workloads. Neil Plumridge says he makes it a priority to work out what has to change; that might mean allocating extra resources to a job, allowing more time or changing expectations. The decision may take several days. He also relies on the advice of colleagues, saying his peers coach each other with business problems. "Just a fresh pair of eyes over an issue can help," he says.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: Executive stress is not confined to big organisations. Vanessa Stoykov has been running her own advertising and public relations business for seven years, specialising in work for financial and professional services firms. Evolution Media has grown so fast that it debuted on the BRW Fast 100 list of fastest-growing small enterprises last year – just after Stoykov had her first child. Stoykov thrives on the mental stimulation of running her own business. "Like everyone, I have the occasional day when I think my head's going to blow off," she says. Because of the growth phase the business is in, Stoykov has to concentrate on short-term stress relief –weekends in the mountains, the occasional "mental health" day – rather than delegating more work. She says: "We're hiring more people, but you need to train them, teach them about the culture and the clients, so it's actually more work rather than less."

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: Identify the causes: Jan Elsnera, Melbourne psychologist who specialises in executive coaching, says thriving on a demanding workload is typical of senior executives and other high-potential business people. She says there is no one-size-fits-all approach to stress: some people work best with high-adrenalin periods followed by quieter patches, while others thrive under sustained pressure. "We could take urine and blood hormonal measures and pass a judgment of whether someone's physiologically stressed or not," she says. "But that's not going to give us an indicator of what their experience of stress is, and what the emotional and cognitive impacts of stress are going to be."

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: Eisner's practice is informed by a movement known as positive psychology, a school of thought that argues "positive" experiences – feeling engaged, challenged, and that one is making a contribution to something meaningful – do not balance out negative ones such as stress; instead, they help people increase their resilience over time. Good stress, or positive experiences of being challenged and rewarded, is thus cumulative in the same way as bad stress. Elsner says many of the senior business people she coaches are relying more on regulating bad stress through methods such as meditation and yoga. She points to research showing that meditation can alter the biochemistry of the brain and actually help people"retrain" the way their brains and bodies react to stress. "Meditation and yoga enable you to shift the way that your brain reacts, so if you get proficient at it you're in control."

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: The Australian vice-president of AT Kearney, Neil Plumridge, says: "Often stress is caused by our setting unrealistic expectations of ourselves. I'll promise a client I'll do something tomorrow, and then promise another client the same thing, when I really know it's not going to happen.I've put stress on myself when I could have said to the clients: 'Why don't I give that to you in 48 hours?' The client doesn't care." Over-committing is something people experience as an individual problem. We explain it as the result of procrastination or Parkinson's law: that work expands to fill the time available. New research indicates that people may be hard-wired to do it.

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text: A study in the February issue of the Journal of Experimental Psychology shows that people always believe they will be less busy in the future than now. This is a misapprehension, according to the authors of the report, Professor Gal Zauberman, of the University of North Carolina, and Professor John Lynch, of Duke University. "On average, an individual will be just as busy two weeks or a month from now as he or she is today. But that is not how it appears to be in everyday life," they wrote. "People often make commitments long in advance that they would never make if the same commitments required immediate action. That is, they discount future time investments relatively steeply." Why do we perceive a greater "surplus" of time in the future than in the present? The researchers suggest that people underestimate completion times for tasks stretching into the future, and that they are bad at imagining future competition for their time.

questions.title: What is point of view of Jan Elsnera towards work stress

options.text: Medical test can only reveal part of the data needed to cope with stress
Index somebody samples will be abnormal in a stressful experience
Emotional and cognitive affection is superior to physical one
One well designed solution can release all stress

questions.analysis:
题干要求我们了解Jan Elsnera对于工作压力的观点。为了快速找到答案，我们可以使用同义替换和定位技巧。

首先，我们可以通过定位词"Jan Elsnera"来找到相关信息。在原文中，我们可以找到与之对应的句子："Identify the causes: Jan Elsnera, Melbourne psychologist who specialises in executive coaching, says thriving on a demanding workload is typical of senior executives and other high-potential business people." 这句话告诉我们Jan Elsnera是一位心理学家，专门从事高管辅导工作。

接下来，我们需要找到关于工作压力的观点。在原文中，Jan Elsnera提到了不同人对于压力的处理方式不同："She says there is no one-size-fits-all approach to stress: some people work best with high-adrenalin periods followed by quieter patches, while others thrive under sustained pressure." 这句话告诉我们不同人对于压力有不同的处理方式。

最后，我们需要找到Jan Elsnera的具体观点。在原文中，Jan Elsnera提到了医学测试只能揭示应对压力所需数据的一部分："“We could take urine and blood hormonal measures and pass a judgment of whether someone’s physiologically stressed or not,” she says. “But that’s not going to give us an indicator of what their experience of stress is, and what the emotional and cognitive impacts of stress are going to be.”" 这句话告诉我们医学测试只能揭示应对压力所需数据的一部分。

综上所述，根据原文中Jan Elsnera的观点，我们可以得出答案："Medical test can only reveal part of the data needed to cope with stress"，即医学测试只能揭示应对压力所需数据的一部分。所以，答案是A。

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: James Paul Gee, professor of education at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, played his first video game years ago when his six-year-old son Sam was playing Pajama Sam: No Need to Hide When It's Dark Outside. He wanted to play the game so he could support Sam's problem solving. Though Pajama Sam is not an "educational game", it is replete with the types of problems psychologists study when they study thinking and learning. When he saw how well the game held Sam's attention, he wondered what sort of beast a more mature video game might be.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: Video and computer games, like many other popular, entertaining and addicting kid's activities, are looked down upon by many parents as time-wasters, and worse, parents think that these games rot the brain. Violent video games are readily blamed by the media and some experts as the reason why some youth become violent or commit extreme anti-social behavior. Recent content analyses of video games show that as many as 89% of games contain some violent content, but there is no form of aggressive content for 70% of popular games. Many scientists and psychologists, like James Paul Gee, find that video games actually have many benefits –the main one being making kids smart. Video games may actually teach kids high-level thinking skills that they will need in the future.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: "Video games change your brain," according to University of Wisconsin psychologist Shawn Green. Video games change the brain's physical structure the same way as do learning to read, playing the piano, or navigating using a map. Much like exercise can build muscle, the powerful combination of concentration and rewarding surges of neurotransmitters like dopamine, which strengthens neural circuits, can build the player's brain.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: Video games give your child's brain a real workout. In many video games, the skills required to win involve abstract and high level thinking. These skills are not even taught at school. Some of the mental skills trained by video games include: following instructions, problem solving, logic, hand-eye coordination, fine motor and spatial skills. Research also suggests that people can learn iconic, spatial, and visual attention skills from video games. There have been even studies with adults showing that experience with video games is related to better surgical skills. Jacob Benjamin, doctor from Beth Israel Medical Center NY, found a direct link between skill at video gaming and skill at keyhole or laparoscopic surgery. Also, a reason given by experts as to why fighter pilots of today are more skillful is that this generation's pilots are being weaned on video games.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: The players learn to manage resources that are limited, and decide the best use of resources, the same way as in real life. In strategy games, for instance, while developing a city, an unexpected surprise like an enemy might emerge. This forces the player to be flexible and quickly change tactics. Sometimes the player does this almost every second of the game giving the brain a real workout. According to researchers at the University of Rochester, led by Daphne Bavelier, a cognitive scientist, games simulating stressful events such as those found in battle or action games could be a training tool for real-world situations. The study suggests that playing action video games primes the brain to make quick decisions. Video games can be used to train soldiers and surgeons, according to the study. Steven Johnson, author of

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: Everything Bad is Good For You: How Today's Popular Culture, says gamers must deal with immediate problems while keeping their long-term goals on their horizon. Young gamers force themselves to read to get instructions, follow storylines of games, and get information from the game texts.

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text: James Paul Gee, professor of education at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, says that playing a video game is similar to working through a science problem Like students in a laboratory, gamers must come up with a hypothesis. For example, players in some games constantly try out combinations of weapons and powers to use to defeat an enemy. If one does not work, they change hypothesis and try the next one. Video games are goal-driven experiences, says Gee, which are fundamental to learning. Also, using math skills is important to win in many games that involve quantitative analysis like managing resources. In higher levels of a game, players usually fail the first time around, but they keep on trying until they succeed and move on to the next level.

paragraphs.label: H
paragraphs.text: Many games are played online and involve cooperation with other online players in order to win. Video and computer games also help children gain self-confidence and many games are based on history, city building, and governance and so on. Such games indirectly teach children about aspects of life on earth.

paragraphs.label: I
paragraphs.text: In an upcoming study in the journal Current Biology, authors Daphne Bavelier, Alexandre Pouget, and C. Shawn Green report that video games could provide a potent training regimen for speeding up reactions in many types of real-life situations. The researchers tested dozens of 18- to 25-year-olds who were not ordinarily video game players. They split the subjects into two groups. One group played 50 hours of the fast-paced action video games "Call of Duty 2"and "Unreal Tournament," and the other group played 50 hours of the slow-moving strategy game "The Sims 2." After this training period, all of the subjects were asked to make quick decisions in several tasks designed by the researchers. The action game players were up to 25 percent faster at coming to a conclusion and answered just as many questions correctly as their strategy game playing peers.

Use the information in the passage to match the people (listed A-F) with opinions or deeds below.
Write the appropriate letters, A-F, in boxes 36-40 on your answer sheet.

questions.title: Brain is ready to make decisions without hesitation when players are immersed in playing stressful games.
questions.analysis:
这道题要求我们找出在玩具游戏时，大脑会毫不犹豫地做出决策的相关信息。我们可以通过在原文中寻找与题干中关键词的同义表达来定位答案。

在原文中，我们可以找到与"Brain"相关的信息。原文中提到了"Video games change your brain"，这意味着玩电子游戏会改变大脑。这与题干中的"Brain is ready to make decisions"相符。

接下来，我们需要找到与"players"相关的信息。原文中提到了"the player’s brain"，这指的是玩家的大脑。这与题干中的"players"相符。

最后，我们需要找到与"immersed"相关的信息。原文中提到了"the powerful combination of concentration"，这意味着专注力。这与题干中的"immersed"相符。

综上所述，根据题干中的关键词，我们可以得出答案"Shawn Green"。

central_sentences.sentence: “Video games change your brain,” according to University of Wisconsin psychologist Shawn Green
central_sentences.translation: 根据威斯康星大学心理学家肖恩·格林的说法，“电子游戏会改变你的大脑”。

central_sentences.sentence: Much like exercise can build muscle, the powerful combination of concentration and rewarding surges of neurotransmitters like dopamine, which strengthens neural circuits, can build the player’s brain
central_sentences.translation: 就像锻炼可以增强肌肉一样，专注和奖励性的神经递质（如多巴胺）的强大组合可以增强神经回路，从而增强玩家的大脑。

target_sen.target_sen: “Video games change your brain,” according to University of Wisconsin psychologist Shawn Green.
Video games change the brain’s physical structure the same way as do learning to read, playing the piano, or navigating using a map.

Much like exercise can build muscle, the powerful combination of concentration and rewarding surges of neurotransmitters like dopamine, which strengthens neural circuits, can build the player’s brain.

questions.title: Video games as other daily life skills alter the brain's physical structure.
questions.analysis: 这道题要求我们找出与视频游戏改变大脑的物理结构相关的信息。在原文中，提到了一项即将发表的研究，作者是Daphne Bavelier等人。他们报告说，视频游戏可以提供一种有效的训练方法，可以加快在许多现实生活情境中做出反应的速度。因此，答案是Daphne Bavelier。

central_sentences.sentence: In an upcoming study in the journal Current Biology, authors Daphne Bavelier, Alexandre Pouget, and C. Shawn Green report that video games could provide a potent training regimen for speeding up reactions in many types of real-life situations
central_sentences.translation: 在即将发表在《当前生物学》杂志上的一项研究中，作者达芙妮·巴韦利尔、亚历山大·普吉和C.肖恩·格林报告称，电子游戏可以成为一种有效的训练方法，加快在许多现实生活情境中的反应速度。

target_sen.target_sen: In an upcoming study in the journal Current Biology, authors Daphne Bavelier, Alexandre Pouget, and C. Shawn Green report that video games could provide a potent training regimen for speeding up reactions in many types of real-life situations.

One group played 50 hours of the fast-paced action video games “Call of Duty 2”and “Unreal Tournament,” and the other group played 50 hours of the slow-moving strategy game “The Sims 2.” After this training period, all of the subjects were asked to make quick decisions in several tasks designed by the researchers.

In an upcoming study in the journal Current Biology, authors Daphne Bavelier, Alexandre Pouget, and C. Shawn Green report that video games could provide a potent training regimen for speeding up reactions in many types of real-life situations.

questions.title: The purpose-motivated experience that video games offer plays an essential role in studying.
questions.analysis: 题干中提到了视频游戏提供的以目的为动力的体验在学习中起着重要作用。我们可以在原文中找到相关信息。原文中提到，许多科学家和心理学家，如James Paul Gee，发现视频游戏实际上有很多好处，其中一个主要好处就是让孩子们变聪明。因此，James Paul Gee是与题干中提到的目的驱动的体验和学习相关的人物。

central_sentences.sentence: Many scientists and psychologists, like James Paul Gee, find that video games actually have many benefits –the main one being making kids smart
central_sentences.translation: 许多科学家和心理学家，如詹姆斯·保罗·吉（James Paul Gee），发现视频游戏实际上有许多好处-其中最主要的好处是让孩子聪明。

target_sen.target_sen: Many scientists and psychologists, like James Paul Gee, find that video games actually have many benefits –the main one being making kids smart.

Video and computer games, like many other popular, entertaining and addicting kid’s activities, are looked down upon by many parents as time-wasters, and worse, parents think that these games rot the brain.
Many scientists and psychologists, like James Paul Gee, find that video games actually have many benefits –the main one being making kids smart.

questions.title: Players are good at tackling prompt issues with future intensions.
questions.analysis:
这道题要求匹配题干中的信息与原文中的段落。题干中提到"Players are good at tackling prompt issues with future intensions."，我们可以将其中的关键词进行同义替换，即"Players"可以替换为"gamers"，"tackling"可以替换为"deal with"，"prompt issues"可以替换为"immediate problems"，"future intensions"可以替换为"long-term goals"。

在原文中，我们可以找到与这些同义表达相对应的句子。在原文中的第一句话中，提到了"gamers must deal with immediate problems while keeping their long-term goals on their horizon"，这与题干中的信息相对应。

因此，根据题干中的信息与原文的对应，我们可以得出答案为"Steven Johnson"。

central_sentences.sentence: Everything Bad is Good For You: How Today’s Popular Culture, says gamers must deal with immediate problems while keeping their long-term goals on their horizon
central_sentences.translation: 《一切坏事都对你有好处：当今流行文化的力量》说，游戏玩家必须在解决眼前问题的同时，保持对长期目标的远见。

target_sen.target_sen: Everything Bad is Good For You: How Today’s Popular Culture, says gamers must deal with immediate problems while keeping their long-term goals on their horizon.
Young gamers force themselves to read to get instructions, follow storylines of games, and get information from the game texts.

questions.title: It helps children broaden their horizon in many aspects and gain self-confidence.
questions.analysis: 题干中提到了"帮助孩子在许多方面拓宽视野并获得自信"，而原文中提到"视频和电脑游戏也帮助孩子们获得自信"，这是一个同义替换。因此，我们可以确定这是作者的观点。所以答案是"The writer’s opinion"。

central_sentences.sentence: Video and computer games also help children gain self-confidence and many games are based on history, city building, and governance and so on
central_sentences.translation: 视频和电脑游戏还可以帮助孩子们获得自信，许多游戏都基于历史、城市建设和治理等主题。

target_sen.target_sen: Video and computer games also help children gain self-confidence and many games are based on history, city building, and governance and so on.
Such games indirectly teach children about aspects of life on earth.
